agent_output:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). The synchronous Notification fan-in creates a cascading failure risk across all five callers. The god endpoint on Customer Service violates interface segregation and creates bandwidth/security concerns. The HTTP-based background jobs conflate synchronous request/response with asynchronous work. Remediation should be phased: stabilize first (break the shared DB and notification fan-in), then refactor (reporting, customer endpoint, job infrastructure).",

  "architecture": {
    "components": [
      { "name": "Order Service", "responsibility": "Order lifecycle management", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Courier, database read directly by Reporting" },
      { "name": "Courier Service", "responsibility": "Courier dispatch and tracking", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Order Service instead of communicating via API/events" },
      { "name": "Notification Service", "responsibility": "Send notifications across all channels", "boundaries": "Technically intact but is a synchronous bottleneck — every caller blocks on it" },
      { "name": "Payment Service", "responsibility": "Payment processing and refunds", "boundaries": "Coupled to Notification synchronously" },
      { "name": "Customer Service", "responsibility": "Customer profile, preferences, addresses, payment methods, order history", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — god endpoint exposes entire aggregate graph with no segmentation" },
      { "name": "Reporting Service", "responsibility": "Generate business reports", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — bypasses Order Service API and reads directly from its database tables" },
      { "name": "Cron Job Runner", "responsibility": "Trigger background processing", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — uses HTTP endpoints meant for synchronous request/response to trigger async work" }
    ],
    "interfaces": [
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Courier Service", "contract": "Shared PostgreSQL database with schema prefixes", "coupling_type": "database coupling (worst kind — schema, query, and data model coupling)" },
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Payment Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Order Service DB", "contract": "Direct SQL reads against Order tables", "coupling_type": "database coupling (read-only but still schema-coupled)" },
      { "from": "Cron Runner", "to": "Various Services", "contract": "HTTP GET/POST to /api/v1/jobs/* endpoints", "coupling_type": "protocol misuse — synchronous HTTP for async work" },
      { "from": "Any Consumer", "to": "Customer Service", "contract": "GET /api/v1/everything — returns entire customer graph", "coupling_type": "stamp coupling (consumers receive far more data than needed)" }
    ],
    "data_flow": "Order and Courier services share a single PostgreSQL instance with schema prefixes (order_* and courier_*), meaning either service can read/write the other's data with a simple schema change. Reporting Service connects directly to Order's database, bypassing any API contract. Five services synchronously call Notification Service for every event, creating a fan-in bottleneck. Customer Service returns the full object graph on every request regardless of what the caller needs. Background jobs are triggered via HTTP by an external cron, meaning job execution is constrained by HTTP timeouts and the jobs compete with user-facing traffic for connection pool and thread resources.",
    "patterns_used": []
  },

  "domain_model": {
    "bounded_contexts": [
      { "name": "Order Management", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Order", "OrderLine", "OrderStatus"] },
      { "name": "Courier & Delivery", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Courier", "Delivery", "Route"] },
      { "name": "Customer Management", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Customer", "Address", "Preference"] },
      { "name": "Payment", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Payment", "Refund", "PaymentMethod"] },
      { "name": "Notification", "classification": "generic", "aggregates": ["Notification", "Template", "Channel"] },
      { "name": "Reporting", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Report", "ReportSchedule"] }
    ],
    "context_map": [
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Courier & Delivery", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Customer-Supplier via domain events; CURRENTLY shared database (no boundary)" },
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Reporting", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Published Language (read model/API); CURRENTLY direct DB access (Conformist to schema)" },
      { "upstream": "All Contexts", "downstream": "Notification", "relationship": "SHOULD BE async domain events; CURRENTLY synchronous RPC (tight temporal coupling)" }
    ]
  },

  "trade_offs": [
    {
      "decision": "Split shared Order/Courier database into separate databases",
      "gains": "Independent deployability, schema evolution without cross-service coordination, clear ownership, eliminates hidden coupling",
      "sacrifices": "Requires data migration, temporary dual-write period, eventual consistency where joins currently exist, short-term development cost",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Enforce schema-level read-only permissions (band-aid — still schema-coupled)", "Shared database with strict API views (moderate — reduces but doesn't eliminate coupling)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace synchronous Notification calls with async event bus",
      "gains": "Eliminates cascading failures, callers no longer block on notification delivery, Notification Service can scale independently, enables retry/dead-letter patterns",
      "sacrifices": "Eventual consistency for notification delivery, need to build event infrastructure, debugging becomes harder (distributed tracing needed)",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Fire-and-forget HTTP (partial fix — still HTTP coupling)", "Sidecar/outbox pattern per service (more complex but stronger guarantees)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Split Customer /everything endpoint into purpose-specific endpoints",
      "gains": "Reduced payload sizes, better security (payment methods not leaked to services that don't need them), cacheable responses, follows Interface Segregation Principle",
      "sacrifices": "More endpoints to maintain, consumers must update their integrations, potential N+1 if consumers need multiple sub-resources",
      "alternatives_considered": ["GraphQL (flexible but adds complexity and a new paradigm)", "BFF pattern per consumer (good for frontend, overkill for service-to-service)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Move background jobs to a proper job queue",
      "gains": "Retry semantics, backpressure, job isolation from user traffic, proper timeout handling, dead-letter queues, observability",
      "sacrifices": "New infrastructure dependency (Redis/RabbitMQ/SQS), migration effort, team needs to learn job queue patterns",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Keep HTTP but add async wrapper (lipstick on a pig — still HTTP timeout constrained)", "Database-backed job queue (simpler, no new infra, but less capable)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Give Reporting its own read replica or materialized views via events",
      "gains": "Order Service can evolve schema freely, Reporting gets optimized read model, no query contention on Order's primary database",
      "sacrifices": "Data staleness (eventual consistency), additional infrastructure, need to build projection/sync mechanism",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Read replica of Order DB (quick win but still schema-coupled)", "Order Service exposes reporting-specific API (simple but may not scale for complex queries)"]
    }
  ],

  "flexibility_points": [
    { "area": "Event bus for notifications", "evolution_path": "Start with a simple message broker (SQS/Redis Streams). This becomes the backbone for all inter-service communication over time. Other synchronous calls can migrate to events incrementally." },
    { "area": "Customer API segmentation", "evolution_path": "Split into /profile, /addresses, /payment-methods, /orders, /preferences. Each can evolve independently. Can later add GraphQL as a gateway layer if consumer needs become more dynamic." },
    { "area": "Reporting read model", "evolution_path": "Start with a CDC (Change Data Capture) pipeline from Order DB to a Reporting-owned store. Can evolve into a full data warehouse or event-sourced projection as reporting needs grow." },
    { "area": "Job infrastructure", "evolution_path": "Start with a database-backed job queue (e.g., pg-boss, Oban, or Sidekiq) to minimize new infrastructure. Migrate to a dedicated broker (SQS, RabbitMQ) when scale demands it." }
  ],

  "rigidity_points": [
    { "area": "Order/Courier database split", "reason": "This is the hardest change and must be done carefully with a migration plan. Once split, the boundary is rigid by design — services cannot casually query each other's data. This rigidity is desirable." },
    { "area": "Event contracts", "reason": "Once services communicate via events, the event schema becomes a contract. Must be versioned and governed. Schema registry or similar tooling is non-negotiable." },
    { "area": "Notification Service's internal routing", "reason": "Notification should remain a single service that owns channel routing (email, SMS, push). The rigidity here prevents notification logic from scattering across producers." }
  ],

  "risks": [
    { "risk": "Shared DB split causes data inconsistency during migration — Order and Courier may diverge if dual-write isn't carefully orchestrated", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Use Strangler Fig pattern: new writes go through APIs, old direct-DB paths are deprecated incrementally. Feature flags control cutover. Run both paths in parallel with reconciliation checks." },
    { "risk": "Notification Service outage currently takes down all 5 calling services (cascading failure)", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Immediate: add circuit breakers and timeouts to all synchronous Notification calls. Medium-term: migrate to async events. This is the highest-ROI quick fix." },
    { "risk": "Reporting Service queries cause lock contention or performance degradation on Order Service's primary database", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Immediate: point Reporting at a read replica. Medium-term: build a dedicated reporting data store populated via CDC or events." },
    { "risk": "Customer /everything endpoint leaks payment method data to services that don't need it (security/PCI exposure)", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Audit all consumers of /everything. Split endpoint immediately. Payment method data should only be accessible to Payment Service and authorized frontends." },
    { "risk": "HTTP-based background jobs time out on long-running refund processing, causing partial completions with no retry", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Move to job queue with at-least-once delivery. Ensure refund processing is idempotent. Add dead-letter queue for failed jobs." },
    { "risk": "Team lacks experience with event-driven patterns, leading to poorly designed events and debugging difficulty", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Start small — migrate Notification to async first as a learning exercise. Invest in distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) before expanding event usage. Establish event design guidelines." },
    { "risk": "Reporting Service's direct SQL queries break silently when Order Service modifies its schema", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Immediate: add contract tests that verify Reporting's queries against Order's schema. This is a stopgap until the proper read model is built." }
  ],

  "implementation_guidance": {
    "recommended_approach": "Phase the remediation into three waves, prioritized by blast radius and risk. WAVE 1 (Stabilize, weeks 1-4): Add circuit breakers to all Notification Service callers, point Reporting at a read replica of Order DB, audit Customer /everything consumers and identify required sub-resources. WAVE 2 (Decouple, weeks 5-12): Introduce a message broker and migrate Notification to event-driven, split Customer endpoint into purpose-specific APIs, replace HTTP-triggered jobs with a proper job queue. WAVE 3 (Separate, weeks 13-24): Split Order/Courier shared database using Strangler Fig, build Reporting's own read model via CDC, decommission direct DB access paths. Each wave is independently valuable — do not skip Wave 1 quick-wins while planning Wave 2.",
    "files_to_create_or_modify": [
      "All services calling Notification: add circuit breaker middleware (e.g., resilience4j, opossum, polly)",
      "Customer Service: split /api/v1/everything into /api/v2/customers/{id}/profile, /addresses, /payment-methods, /order-history, /preferences",
      "Order Service: define domain events (OrderCreated, OrderStatusChanged, OrderCancelled) and publish to message broker",
      "Courier Service: subscribe to Order domain events instead of reading Order tables directly",
      "Reporting Service: new data store schema, CDC pipeline configuration, decommission direct Order DB connection",
      "Background job services: replace /api/v1/jobs/* endpoints with job queue workers, add idempotency keys",
      "Infrastructure: message broker setup (SQS/RabbitMQ), job queue setup, read replica configuration",
      "All services: add OpenTelemetry distributed tracing before expanding async communication"
    ],
    "patterns_to_follow": "Strangler Fig for database split (parallel run with reconciliation). Outbox Pattern for reliable event publishing (write event to local DB table, relay to broker). Circuit Breaker on all synchronous cross-service calls. Interface Segregation for Customer API split. Change Data Capture for Reporting read model. Idempotent Consumer for all event handlers and job processors.",
    "pitfalls_to_avoid": [
      "Do NOT attempt a big-bang database split — use Strangler Fig with incremental migration",
      "Do NOT introduce an event bus without distributed tracing — you will lose the ability to debug cross-service flows",
      "Do NOT just add more schema prefixes to 'fix' the shared database — prefixes are not boundaries",
      "Do NOT make Notification fire-and-forget over HTTP as a shortcut — you lose delivery guarantees without gaining decoupling",
      "Do NOT split Customer endpoint without first mapping all consumers — you will break unknown callers",
      "Do NOT keep HTTP job endpoints 'for backward compatibility' after migration — they will be used and you will have two systems",
      "Do NOT build a custom event bus — use proven infrastructure (SQS, RabbitMQ, Kafka). The team's energy should go into domain modeling, not infrastructure reinvention"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 82,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Anti-pattern identification — all five issues are well-documented anti-patterns with clear remediation paths",
      "Prioritization — shared DB and notification fan-in are unambiguously the highest-risk items",
      "Circuit breaker as immediate mitigation — this is a standard, low-risk quick win",
      "Strangler Fig for database split — proven migration pattern for exactly this situation"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Specific message broker choice — depends on team expertise, existing infrastructure, and scale requirements (not provided)",
      "CDC tooling choice — Debezium vs custom vs managed service depends on infrastructure context",
      "Timeline estimates — weeks are rough guides; actual effort depends on team size, test coverage, and how tangled the shared DB queries are",
      "Customer endpoint split granularity — exact sub-resource boundaries depend on actual consumer usage patterns which are unknown"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Services are independently deployable (containerized or similar) — if they are a monolith deployed together, the shared DB is less critical to fix first",
      "Team has or can acquire message broker operational expertise within the remediation timeframe",
      "There is no hard real-time requirement on notification delivery (async with seconds of delay is acceptable)",
      "The shared database does not enforce cross-schema foreign keys — if it does, the split is significantly harder",
      "Background jobs do not need sub-second triggering precision (job queue polling latency is acceptable)"
    ]
  }
}

  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). The synchronous Notification fan-in creates a cascading failure risk across all five callers. The god endpoint on Customer Service violates interface segregation

      { "name": "Payment", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Payment", "Refund", "PaymentMethod"] },
      { "name": "Notification", "classification": "generic", "aggregates": ["Notification", "Template", "Channel"] },

  ],

    { "risk": "Notification Service outage currently takes down all 5 calling services (cascading failure)", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Immediate: add circuit breakers and timeouts to all synchronous Notification calls.   ],

WAVE 1 (Stabilize, weeks 1-4): Add circuit breakers to all Notification Service callers, point Reporting at a read replica of Order DB, audit Customer /everything consumers and identify required sub-resources.     ],

Circuit Breaker on all synchronous cross-service calls. Interface Segregation for Customer API split. Change Data Capture for Reporting read model. Idempotent Consumer for all event handlers and job processors.",

      "Do NOT attempt a big-bang database split — use Strangler Fig with incremental migration",
      "Do NOT introduce an event bus without distributed tracing — you will lose the ability to debug cross-service flows",
      "Do NOT just add more schema prefixes to 'fix' the shared database — prefixes are not boundaries",
      "Do NOT make Notification fire-and-forget over HTTP as a shortcut — you lose delivery guarantees without gaining decoupling",
      "Do NOT split Customer endpoint without first mapping all consumers — you will break unknown callers",

trace.result:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). The synchronous Notification fan-in creates a cascading failure risk across all five callers. The god endpoint on Customer Service violates interface segregation and creates bandwidth/security concerns. The HTTP-based background jobs conflate synchronous request/response with asynchronous work. Remediation should be phased: stabilize first (break the shared DB and notification fan-in), then refactor (reporting, customer endpoint, job infrastructure).",

  "architecture": {
    "components": [
      { "name": "Order Service", "responsibility": "Order lifecycle management", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Courier, database read directly by Reporting" },
      { "name": "Courier Service", "responsibility": "Courier dispatch and tracking", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Order Service instead of communicating via API/events" },
      { "name": "Notification Service", "responsibility": "Send notifications across all channels", "boundaries": "Technically intact but is a synchronous bottleneck — every caller blocks on it" },
      { "name": "Payment Service", "responsibility": "Payment processing and refunds", "boundaries": "Coupled to Notification synchronously" },
      { "name": "Customer Service", "responsibility": "Customer profile, preferences, addresses, payment methods, order history", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — god endpoint exposes entire aggregate graph with no segmentation" },
      { "name": "Reporting Service", "responsibility": "Generate business reports", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — bypasses Order Service API and reads directly from its database tables" },
      { "name": "Cron Job Runner", "responsibility": "Trigger background processing", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — uses HTTP endpoints meant for synchronous request/response to trigger async work" }
    ],
    "interfaces": [
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Courier Service", "contract": "Shared PostgreSQL database with schema prefixes", "coupling_type": "database coupling (worst kind — schema, query, and data model coupling)" },
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Payment Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Order Service DB", "contract": "Direct SQL reads against Order tables", "coupling_type": "database coupling (read-only but still schema-coupled)" },
      { "from": "Cron Runner", "to": "Various Services", "contract": "HTTP GET/POST to /api/v1/jobs/* endpoints", "coupling_type": "protocol misuse — synchronous HTTP for async work" },
      { "from": "Any Consumer", "to": "Customer Service", "contract": "GET /api/v1/everything — returns entire customer graph", "coupling_type": "stamp coupling (consumers receive far more data than needed)" }
    ],
    "data_flow": "Order and Courier services share a single PostgreSQL instance with schema prefixes (order_* and courier_*), meaning either service can read/write the other's data with a simple schema change. Reporting Service connects directly to Order's database, bypassing any API contract. Five services synchronously call Notification Service for every event, creating a fan-in bottleneck. Customer Service returns the full object graph on every request regardless of what the caller needs. Background jobs are triggered via HTTP by an external cron, meaning job execution is constrained by HTTP timeouts and the jobs compete with user-facing traffic for connection pool and thread resources.",
    "patterns_used": []
  },

  "domain_model": {
    "bounded_contexts": [
      { "name": "Order Management", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Order", "OrderLine", "OrderStatus"] },
      { "name": "Courier & Delivery", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Courier", "Delivery", "Route"] },
      { "name": "Customer Management", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Customer", "Address", "Preference"] },
      { "name": "Payment", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Payment", "Refund", "PaymentMethod"] },
      { "name": "Notification", "classification": "generic", "aggregates": ["Notification", "Template", "Channel"] },
      { "name": "Reporting", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Report", "ReportSchedule"] }
    ],
    "context_map": [
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Courier & Delivery", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Customer-Supplier via domain events; CURRENTLY shared database (no boundary)" },
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Reporting", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Published Language (read model/API); CURRENTLY direct DB access (Conformist to schema)" },
      { "upstream": "All Contexts", "downstream": "Notification", "relationship": "SHOULD BE async domain events; CURRENTLY synchronous RPC (tight temporal coupling)" }
    ]
  },

  "trade_offs": [
    {
      "decision": "Split shared Order/Courier database into separate databases",
      "gains": "Independent deployability, schema evolution without cross-service coordination, clear ownership, eliminates hidden coupling",
      "sacrifices": "Requires data migration, temporary dual-write period, eventual consistency where joins currently exist, short-term development cost",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Enforce schema-level read-only permissions (band-aid — still schema-coupled)", "Shared database with strict API views (moderate — reduces but doesn't eliminate coupling)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace synchronous Notification calls with async event bus",
      "gains": "Eliminates cascading failures, callers no longer block on notification delivery, Notification Service can scale independently, enables retry/dead-letter patterns",
      "sacrifices": "Eventual consistency for notification delivery, need to build event infrastructure, debugging becomes harder (distributed tracing needed)",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Fire-and-forget HTTP (partial fix — still HTTP coupling)", "Sidecar/outbox pattern per service (more complex but stronger guarantees)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Split Customer /everything endpoint into purpose-specific endpoints",
      "gains": "Reduced payload sizes, better security (payment methods not leaked to services that don't need them), cacheable responses, follows Interface Segregation Principle",
      "sacrifices": "More endpoints to maintain, consumers must update their integrations, potential N+1 if consumers need multiple sub-resources",
      "alternatives_considered": ["GraphQL (flexible but adds complexity and a new paradigm)", "BFF pattern per consumer (good for frontend, overkill for service-to-service)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Move background jobs to a proper job queue",
      "gains": "Retry semantics, backpressure, job isolation from user traffic, proper timeout handling, dead-letter queues, observability",
      "sacrifices": "New infrastructure dependency (Redis/RabbitMQ/SQS), migration effort, team needs to learn job queue patterns",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Keep HTTP but add async wrapper (lipstick on a pig — still HTTP timeout constrained)", "Database-backed job queue (simpler, no new infra, but less capable)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Give Reporting its own read replica or materialized views via events",
      "gains": "Order Service can evolve schema freely, Reporting gets optimized read model, no query contention on Order's primary database",
      "sacrifices": "Data staleness (eventual consistency), additional infrastructure, need to build projection/sync mechanism",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Read replica of Order DB (quick win but still schema-coupled)", "Order Service exposes reporting-specific API (simple but may not scale for complex queries)"]
    }
  ],

  "flexibility_points": [
    { "area": "Event bus for notifications", "evolution_path": "Start with a simple message broker (SQS/Redis Streams). This becomes the backbone for all inter-service communication over time. Other synchronous calls can migrate to events incrementally." },
    { "area": "Customer API segmentation", "evolution_path": "Split into /profile, /addresses, /payment-methods, /orders, /preferences. Each can evolve independently. Can later add GraphQL as a gateway layer if consumer needs become more dynamic." },
    { "area": "Reporting read model", "evolution_path": "Start with a CDC (Change Data Capture) pipeline from Order DB to a Reporting-owned store. Can evolve into a full data warehouse or event-sourced projection as reporting needs grow." },
    { "area": "Job infrastructure", "evolution_path": "Start with a database-backed job queue (e.g., pg-boss, Oban, or Sidekiq) to minimize new infrastructure. Migrate to a dedicated broker (SQS, RabbitMQ) when scale demands it." }
  ],

  "rigidity_points": [
    { "area": "Order/Courier database split", "reason": "This is the hardest change and must be done carefully with a migration plan. Once split, the boundary is rigid by design — services cannot casually query each other's data. This rigidity is desirable." },
    { "area": "Event contracts", "reason": "Once services communicate via events, the event schema becomes a contract. Must be versioned and governed. Schema registry or similar tooling is non-negotiable." },
    { "area": "Notification Service's internal routing", "reason": "Notification should remain a single service that owns channel routing (email, SMS, push). The rigidity here prevents notification logic from scattering across producers." }
  ],

  "risks": [
    { "risk": "Shared DB split causes data inconsistency during migration — Order and Courier may diverge if dual-write isn't carefully orchestrated", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Use Strangler Fig pattern: new writes go through APIs, old direct-DB paths are deprecated incrementally. Feature flags control cutover. Run both paths in parallel with reconciliation checks." },
    { "risk": "Notification Service outage currently takes down all 5 calling services (cascading failure)", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Immediate: add circuit breakers and timeouts to all synchronous Notification calls. Medium-term: migrate to async events. This is the highest-ROI quick fix." },
    { "risk": "Reporting Service queries cause lock contention or performance degradation on Order Service's primary database", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Immediate: point Reporting at a read replica. Medium-term: build a dedicated reporting data store populated via CDC or events." },
    { "risk": "Customer /everything endpoint leaks payment method data to services that don't need it (security/PCI exposure)", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Audit all consumers of /everything. Split endpoint immediately. Payment method data should only be accessible to Payment Service and authorized frontends." },
    { "risk": "HTTP-based background jobs time out on long-running refund processing, causing partial completions with no retry", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Move to job queue with at-least-once delivery. Ensure refund processing is idempotent. Add dead-letter queue for failed jobs." },
    { "risk": "Team lacks experience with event-driven patterns, leading to poorly designed events and debugging difficulty", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Start small — migrate Notification to async first as a learning exercise. Invest in distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) before expanding event usage. Establish event design guidelines." },
    { "risk": "Reporting Service's direct SQL queries break silently when Order Service modifies its schema", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Immediate: add contract tests that verify Reporting's queries against Order's schema. This is a stopgap until the proper read model is built." }
  ],

  "implementation_guidance": {
    "recommended_approach": "Phase the remediation into three waves, prioritized by blast radius and risk. WAVE 1 (Stabilize, weeks 1-4): Add circuit breakers to all Notification Service callers, point Reporting at a read replica of Order DB, audit Customer /everything consumers and identify required sub-resources. WAVE 2 (Decouple, weeks 5-12): Introduce a message broker and migrate Notification to event-driven, split Customer endpoint into purpose-specific APIs, replace HTTP-triggered jobs with a proper job queue. WAVE 3 (Separate, weeks 13-24): Split Order/Courier shared database using Strangler Fig, build Reporting's own read model via CDC, decommission direct DB access paths. Each wave is independently valuable — do not skip Wave 1 quick-wins while planning Wave 2.",
